meeting__item_information.title_of_item: Authorization to Purchase Replacement Single-Axle Plow Truck for Public Works Street Maintenance

agenda_placement__action_type.action_requested: Approve the purchase of one (1) 2025 single-axle plow truck with dump body, front plow, and underbody scraper for the Public Works Street Division from Northland Truck & Equipment in the amount of $248,765.42 including tax and delivery, and authorize the City Administrator and Public Works Director to execute all necessary purchase documents. Further, direct staff to declare the existing 2006 International single-axle plow truck (Unit #14) as surplus equipment and dispose of it via public auction in accordance with City policy.

staff__presentation_details.presenter_name_title: Daniel R. McAllister, Public Works Director/City Engineer

issue_description__recommendation.summary_of_issue:
The Public Works Department is requesting authorization to purchase a replacement single-axle plow truck for the Street Division as part of the City’s approved 2025 Capital Improvement Plan (CIP). The unit proposed for replacement is Truck #14, a 2006 International 7400 single-axle plow truck with approximately 186,000 miles and over 11,000 engine hours. Over the last three winter seasons, Truck #14 has experienced increasing downtime due to drivetrain, hydraulic, and electrical issues, including repeated failures of the hydraulic control valves for the underbody scraper and salt spreader, corrosion-related wiring problems, and significant rust on the dump body and frame components.

In the 2025 CIP, the City Council previously reviewed and tentatively approved the planned replacement of this unit, with final purchase authorization to be brought back at the time of procurement. Staff has solicited a formal quote through the state cooperative purchasing contract administered by the Minnesota Department of Administration (State Contract #T-642). Utilizing the state contract allows the City to benefit from competitive pricing that has already been publicly bid and vetted, reducing staff time and ensuring compliance with state and local purchasing requirements.

Northland Truck & Equipment, based in Duluth, submitted a complete quote for a 2025 Freightliner 108SD single-axle chassis with a Henderson dump body, front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless steel salt/sand spreader, including installation, calibration, and delivery. The total quoted price, including all applicable taxes and delivery, is $248,765.42. The new truck will be outfitted with updated safety features such as LED lighting, improved operator visibility, and upgraded controls that will improve efficiency during snow and ice operations. If approved at the January 13, 2025 Council meeting, the estimated build and delivery time is 9–11 months, which would place the new truck in service prior to the 2026–2027 snow season.

Due to the age, condition, and maintenance history of Truck #14, staff believes it is no longer cost-effective or reliable enough to continue operating as a frontline plow unit. The replacement is consistent with the City’s fleet replacement schedule, which targets a 15–18 year useful life for heavy plow trucks, depending on condition and usage. The proposed purchase will ensure that the Street Division maintains adequate, reliable equipment to meet the community’s expectations for timely snow and ice control on city streets, cul-de-sacs, and municipal parking lots.
issue_description__recommendation.alternatives_options_effects_on_others_c:
ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED:

1. **Defer replacement for 1–2 more years and continue operating Truck #14**
   - *Pros*: Avoids or delays the immediate capital expenditure of approximately $249,000; allows more time to evaluate potential changes in equipment technology or alternative fuels.
   - *Cons*: Truck #14 has already exceeded the typical life cycle for a front-line plow truck, and the rate of mechanical failures has increased significantly over the last three winters. In 2023–2024 alone, the truck was out of service for a cumulative 21 days during the snow season, requiring staff to reshuffle routes and rely on overtime and contractor assistance. Continuing to operate this aging unit is expected to increase maintenance costs, with Public Works mechanics estimating an additional $8,000–$10,000 per year in parts and labor just to keep it operational. There is also an increased risk of in-route breakdowns during storms, which can delay snow removal and generate resident complaints.

2. **Purchase a used or refurbished plow truck instead of a new truck**
   - *Pros*: Potentially lower upfront cost, depending on availability and condition of used units; may reduce the immediate impact on the Capital Equipment Fund.
   - *Cons*: The used heavy-duty plow truck market is very tight, and units that become available are typically retired from other municipalities or counties after already reaching the end of their useful lives. These vehicles often come with unknown maintenance histories and may require extensive repairs or retrofits to meet our operational needs and safety standards. Additionally, used trucks purchased from out of state can present challenges in terms of parts compatibility and compliance with Minnesota-specific requirements for winter maintenance equipment. Staff’s experience, corroborated by discussions with neighboring cities (Baxter, Little Falls, and Crow Wing County Highway Department), is that used plow trucks tend to have significantly higher lifecycle maintenance costs and shorter remaining service lives.

3. **Outsource additional plowing to private contractors instead of replacing the truck**
   - *Pros*: Reduces the City’s capital investment in heavy equipment; can provide flexibility to scale up or down based on winter severity.
   - *Cons*: The City already uses some contracted assistance for cul-de-sacs and certain parking lots during major storms, but fully replacing a frontline plow route with contractors would increase the annual operating budget and reduce the City’s direct control over response times and quality. Contractors also face staffing and equipment constraints of their own, and may not be available during peak demand periods. Based on recent quotes for contracted plowing, staff estimates that fully outsourcing the equivalent capacity of Truck #14 would cost between $35,000 and $50,000 per year, with no long-term asset to show for the expenditure.

EFFECTS ON OTHERS:

- **Residents and Businesses**: Replacing Truck #14 will help maintain consistent plow coverage on residential routes, school zones, and downtown streets. Reliable equipment translates to more predictable plow start and completion times, which is particularly important for residents who must commute early in the morning and for businesses that rely on clear access for customers and deliveries.

- **Public Works Crews**: Operators have reported increasing frustration with Truck #14 due to frequent mechanical issues, inconsistent hydraulic performance, and cabin heating problems. These issues are especially problematic during overnight and extended storm events, where comfort and reliability directly affect operator fatigue and safety. Upgrading to a newer unit with more ergonomic controls and a more reliable heating/defrost system will support staff morale and reduce fatigue-related errors.

- **Fleet Maintenance Staff**: Mechanics have spent a disproportionate amount of time troubleshooting intermittent electrical and hydraulic issues on Truck #14, which diverts attention from routine preventative maintenance on other equipment. A new truck under warranty will reduce unplanned repair work for at least the first several years of service and allow the Fleet Division to focus on scheduled maintenance and other priority repairs.

ADDITIONAL COMMENTS:

Staff has coordinated with the Finance Department to confirm that this purchase aligns with the adopted 2025 Capital Equipment Plan and that sufficient funds are available in the Public Works Capital Equipment Fund. No rate or tax levy adjustment is required as a result of this purchase; the replacement was anticipated when the 2025 budget was developed. The proposed vendor, Northland Truck & Equipment, has previously supplied plow trucks to the City of Brainerd and surrounding jurisdictions, and staff has had positive experiences with their responsiveness for warranty and parts support.

Staff considered whether to pursue alternative-fuel or hybrid plow trucks as part of this replacement cycle. At this time, fully electric or hybrid heavy-duty plow trucks remain in limited production and are significantly more expensive, with uncertain performance in extended sub-zero conditions. Staff will continue to monitor developments in this area and expects that the next round of replacements, approximately 8–10 years from now, may be better suited for a transition to lower-emission technologies.
issue_description__recommendation.recommended_action_motion: Motion to authorize the purchase of one (1) 2025 Freightliner 108SD single-axle plow truck with Henderson dump body, front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless steel salt/sand spreader for the Public Works Street Division from Northland Truck & Equipment under Minnesota State Contract #T-642 in the amount of $248,765.42, including sales tax and delivery, with funding from the 2025 Public Works Capital Equipment Fund as identified in the adopted Capital Improvement Plan; and to declare the existing 2006 International 7400 plow truck (Unit #14, VIN ending 4821) as surplus property and authorize staff to dispose of it via public auction in accordance with City policy, with sale proceeds returned to the Capital Equipment Fund.

financial_impact.please_explain:
The total cost of the proposed purchase, including chassis, dump body, plow equipment, spreader, installation, calibration, sales tax, and delivery to the Brainerd Public Works facility, is $248,765.42. This figure is based on the formal quote provided by Northland Truck & Equipment utilizing the State of Minnesota cooperative purchasing contract (Contract #T-642). The quoted amount is within the estimate included in the adopted 2025 Capital Improvement Plan, which anticipated a cost of $255,000 for this replacement.

This expenditure is fully budgeted in the 2025 Public Works Capital Equipment Fund (Fund 401), under line item 401-43100-5800 (Machinery & Equipment). The Finance Director has confirmed that, as of the beginning of the 2025 fiscal year, there is sufficient balance in the fund to cover this purchase without requiring a budget amendment. The replacement of Truck #14 was specifically identified during the 2024 budget workshops and was factored into the 2025 levy and long-term equipment replacement schedule.

There is no direct impact on user fees or special assessments associated with this purchase. The long-term financial benefit of replacing the truck at this time is expected to come from reduced maintenance and downtime costs. Over the last three fiscal years, repair and maintenance expenses for Truck #14 have averaged approximately $9,300 per year, compared to an average of $3,500–$4,000 per year for newer plow trucks in the fleet. While routine maintenance will still be required, staff anticipates a reduction in unplanned repair costs and mechanic labor hours during the first 7–10 years of the new truck’s service life, particularly while major components remain under manufacturer warranty.

The City also expects to recover a modest portion of the investment through the sale of the surplus 2006 International 7400. Based on recent auction results for similar units in central Minnesota, staff conservatively estimates a resale value in the range of $12,000–$18,000, depending on final condition at the time of sale and market demand. Any proceeds from the auction will be returned to the Public Works Capital Equipment Fund and will help support future equipment replacements.

In summary, while this is a significant capital purchase, it has been planned for in the City’s long-range financial strategy, is fully budgeted for the current year, and is expected to reduce operational risk and maintenance costs associated with an aging and increasingly unreliable plow truck.
